skill.name: managing-worktrees
skill.description: Guides AI assistants through Git worktree creation, listing, status review, and cleanup for parallel development workflows.
skill.summary: Manage Git worktrees for isolated branches, hotfixes, pull request reviews, and cleanup.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command and filesystem indicators, but review shows they are Git worktree examples inside SKILL.md. The skill is not malicious and shows no prompt injection, network access, credential access, or obfuscation. It remains medium risk because it grants Bash use and recommends commands that create, remove, prune, and force-remove worktrees.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Mutating Git Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill uses Bash-enabled Git workflows that create worktrees, fetch pull request branches, remove worktrees, and prune stale references. These commands are legitimate for the skill purpose, but they can change repository state and local filesystem contents.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit Git worktree operations in fenced examples and workflow instructions. They match the skill purpose, but their effects on local repository state are real.

medium_findings.title: Force Removal Can Discard Local Work
medium_findings.description: The skill documents force removal for worktrees with uncommitted changes. This is a normal Git option, but unsafe use can delete local worktree content before users preserve changes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The force-removal command and warning are directly present. The risk is clear operational data loss rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Relative Worktree Paths Flagged as Traversal
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged '../worktrees' examples as path traversal. In context, these paths are Git worktree placement conventions, not attempts to access sensitive directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relative paths consistently point to a worktree directory outside the repository. No sensitive file target or user-controlled path expansion is shown.

low_findings.title: Non-Security Static Pattern Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance static findings appear to be false positives from prose and Git listing examples. No cryptographic API, credential access, or host reconnaissance behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no crypto implementation and no system inventory commands. The referenced lines are metadata, path guidance, and cleanup advice.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Worktree Cleanup Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented force-removal pattern can remove a worktree even when it has uncommitted changes. Assistants using this skill should require user confirmation and review status before running it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact force-removal command is shown. The risk depends on user approval and repository state, but the destructive capability is real.

content.value_statement: Parallel Git work can become messy when branches share one working directory. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through worktree creation, review, and cleanup.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Git worktrees instead of normal branch switching.
Provides commands for creating worktrees from existing branches or new branches.
Lists active worktrees and describes clean, dirty, detached, and prunable states.
Outlines cleanup steps for merged worktrees and stale references.
Supports isolated hotfix and pull request review workflows.
limitations: It depends on Git and Bash access in the local environment.
It does not validate every branch name or filesystem path automatically.
Cleanup commands can remove local work if users approve unsafe actions.
It is guidance for worktree workflows, not a full Git safety policy.

use_cases.title: Parallel feature development
use_cases.description: Create a separate worktree for a second feature while preserving current branch state.

use_cases.title: Emergency hotfix isolation
use_cases.description: Start a clean hotfix worktree from the main branch without stashing active work.

use_cases.title: Pull request review
use_cases.description: Fetch and inspect a pull request branch in a temporary worktree.

prompt_templates.title: List my worktrees
prompt_templates.prompt: Show my active Git worktrees and summarize which ones have uncommitted changes.

prompt_templates.title: Create a feature worktree
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a worktree for the feature branch named feature/dashboard, and tell me how to switch into it.

prompt_templates.title: Review a pull request separately
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a temporary worktree for reviewing pull request 123, then list the cleanup steps after review.

prompt_templates.title: Plan safe worktree cleanup
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze my worktrees, identify merged branches, show a dry-run cleanup plan, and avoid deleting anything with uncommitted changes.

output_examples.input: I need to work on dashboard while keeping my auth branch open.
output_examples.output: The skill would propose a separate dashboard worktree, name the target branch and path, then explain how to enter the new directory.

output_examples.input: Clean up worktrees for branches already merged to main.
output: Lists candidate worktrees and their branches.
Flags any uncommitted changes before removal.

output_examples.input: Review PR 123 without touching my current checkout.
output_examples.output: The skill would describe fetching the PR branch into a temporary local branch, creating a review worktree, and removing it after review.

best_practices: Review worktree status before running removal or force-removal commands.
Use predictable paths outside the repository, such as a shared worktrees directory.
Clean up merged worktrees regularly after confirming branches are no longer needed.
anti_patterns: Do not force-remove a worktree before checking for uncommitted changes.
Do not create worktrees inside the same repository checkout.
Do not reuse one branch across multiple active worktrees.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps manage Git worktrees for parallel branch work, hotfixes, pull request reviews, and cleanup.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: The skill provides Bash-based Git workflows. Users should review commands before allowing filesystem changes.

faq.question: Why are worktrees useful?
faq.answer: They let one repository have multiple working directories, each checked out to a different branch.

faq.question: Can it delete files?
faq.answer: Yes. Worktree removal commands can delete worktree directories, especially when force removal is approved.

faq.question: Does it access the network?
faq.answer: No direct network behavior was found, except normal Git fetch examples for pull request review.

faq.question: Is it safe for beginners?
faq.answer: It is useful for beginners if they avoid force cleanup and confirm status before removing worktrees.
